topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. The subject line was terse, a formal announcement about his transition to a new institution. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the library suddenly buzzing with an irritating intensity. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic of my mouse digging into my palms. I pushed back from my desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, a sound that felt deafening in the sudden quiet of the study room. I walked to the window, pulling the blinds partially shut, trying to block out the relentless grey of the afternoon. The weight in my chest felt immense, a suffocating pressure that made drawing a full breath a conscious, labored effort. All the meticulous notes I’d taken, the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, suddenly seemed pointless, dissolving into meaningless ink on the page.

[story 2]

"He's gone," Sarah murmured, not looking up from her textbook. The words hung in the air between us, heavy and undeniable. I nodded slowly, my gaze fixed on the chipped paint of the windowsill. A strange numbness settled over me, a thick, cold blanket muffling the usual frantic thrum of anxiety. I picked up my pen, but the nib felt alien in my grip. My focus, usually sharp enough to dissect complex theories, scattered like dust motes in a sudden draft. I began tracing patterns on the notepad—spirals, then jagged lines—my movements slow, almost robotic. I felt a profound hollowness, as if someone had quietly removed the core of my motivation. Every small task ahead—reading the next chapter, scheduling the next meeting—felt insurmountable, weighted down by an invisible, crushing mass.

[story 3]

I reread the announcement three times. Each time, the phrasing seemed sharper, more final. When I finally closed the laptop, the silence of my dorm room was absolute, amplifying the sound of my own shallow breathing. I slid down onto the edge of my bed, the thin mattress offering little comfort against the churning in my stomach. I didn't bother turning on the lamp; the darkness seemed preferable. My limbs felt heavy, unresponsive, as if gravity had suddenly increased its pull tenfold. A single, hot tear escaped and tracked a slow, hot path down my cheek, quickly absorbed by the fabric of my shirt. The future, which had been a tightly constructed scaffolding of deadlines and expectations, suddenly looked like a structure collapsing into rubble. I just lay there, utterly still, waiting for something to shift, anything to break the inertia.
